coredata.dc:description: Now, metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) are considered to have important scientific applications. Considering the toxicity of these nanomaterials is an important research issue for scientists. In this sense, an important solution to provide lower levels of toxicity and therefore to facilitate unimpeded use in products for human consumption is the green and eco synthesis of nanoparticles. Despite this naive approach, the biological synthesis of metal oxide NPs using microorganisms and plant extracts holds great promise for the fabrication of biocompatible and cost-effective particles with potential applications in healthcare and drug delivery. An important time of ​​attention is deadly cancer and therapies to control it, the introduction of nanotechnology is to improve current therapeutic practices. The oxides and NPs have been perceived as remedial factors with long obsolescence and therapeutic markers, and few immunogenic properties have been punched. Currently, biosynthesized metal oxide nanoparticles have been extensively studied and analyzed for early diagnosis and treatment of cancer, but their efficacy in clinical trials is unknown. It provides a comprehensive overview of recent research on the green synthesis of metal oxide NPs, characterization methods, biomedical and scientific applications globally reported, and indications for further improvements. Moreover, the scientific applications of nanoparticles, including silver (Ag), titanium (Ti), cerium (Ce), selenium (Se), zinc (Zn), iron (Fe), and copper oxide nanomaterials, were highlighted. The review ventilates the importance of environmentally and eco-friendly synthesis of metal oxides in terms of therapeutic, energetic, and environmental pharmaceutical applications using several novel approaches. This study will provide new knowledge on new cost-effective and nanomedicine drugs for future directions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Diverse applications of nanoparticles Created with basic version of BioRender.com.

Nanoparticles (NPs) can be synthesized using physical, chemical, and biological methods. Initially, bulk materials are broken down into powder form. This powder is then processed through techniques such as ball milling (physical), chemical reduction (chemical), or using biological agents like plants or bacteria (biological). Each method offers distinct advantages tailored to specific applications.

Provides a visual representation of the research findings: (I) In-Vitro CT Images: The figure includes (a) in-vitro CT images of lanthanide-doped NaGdF4 up-conversion “nanoclusters” with sizes under 5 nm, suspended in an aqueous solution. The CT attenuation plot (b) is given in Hounsfield units (HU) and shows the relationship between the concentration of NaGdF4 nanoparticles (NPs) in samples ranging from 0.2 to 10 mg/mL, further elucidating the CT contrast effect. (II) Control Group Images Before NP Injection: This section displays (c) a photograph of a nude mouse loaded with gastric cancer MGC-803 cells, (d) an X-ray image, and (e–g) CT images of nude mice from the control group, taken prior to the injection of NPs. (III) CT Images After NP Injection: The figure presents (h–k) CT images of mice after intravenous injection with NaGdF4 up-converting NPs, making use of passive targeting via the Enhanced Permeation and Retention (EPR) effect. (IV) MRI Images After NP Injection: Additionally, (l–n) MRI images of the mice are provided post-injection with NaGdF4 up-converting NPs. The pulse sequence for the MRI images is specified as electromagnetic conversion (EC) = 1/141.7 kHz; repetition time (TR time) = 2000; echo time (TE time) = 65.6/Ef (echo frequency). Parameters of the transverse plane include the pulse sequence (EC=1/141.7 kHz), TR time (2000), and TE time (43.8/Ef). Each image acquisition took approximately 6 h, and the relative value of NaGdF4 UCNPs at 1.5 Tesla is about 4.5 mM s−1. These findings are adapted with permission from reference [325].copyright Royal Society of Chemistry and MDPI and Author permitted [325].
Multifunctional liposome for molecular imaging, drug delivery, and therapy. Copyright from Dove press and author permitted [403].

Describes various applications of near-infrared (NIR-II) emitting fluorophores for structural and functional imaging. The use of these fluorophores allows for imaging of biological structures and processes with improved depth penetration and resolution in the NIR-II window. Here's a summary of each figure: (a) Whole-Body Blood Pool Imaging: This image illustrates whole-body blood pool imaging in a nude mouse using NIR-II emitting Ag2S quantum dots (QDs). NIR-II fluorophores, such as Ag2S QDs, provide enhanced imaging capabilities, allowing for better visualization of blood vessels and circulation within the body. (b) Comparative Lymphatic Imaging: A comparative study is presented between Ag2S QDs emitting in the NIR-II range (with a longer wavelength, 1200 nm) and ICG (Indocyanine Green) emitting in the NIR-I range (835 nm) for in vivo lymphatic imaging. The NIR-II fluorophores offer advantages in terms of deeper tissue penetration and reduced background noise. (c) Two-Color Fluorescence Imaging: This image demonstrates two-color fluorescence imaging using NIR-II emitting fluorophores. The p-EF channel is used for imaging tumor vasculatures, allowing for the visualization of blood vessels supplying the tumor. The CNT channel is employed for imaging the tumor tissue itself, providing detailed information about the tumor. (d) 3D Confocal Image of Cerebral Blood Vessels: A 3D confocal image reveals the intricate network of cerebral blood vessels in a rhesus macaque. The use of NIR-II imaging allows for high-resolution imaging of blood vessels, potentially contributing to studies of neurobiology and vascular health. (e) In Vivo Blood Flow Imaging: This image shows in vivo imaging of blood flow velocities in a rhesus macaque using wide-field NIR-II microscopy. The ability to visualize blood flow dynamics with NIR-II fluorophores can have applications in various physiological and medical research studies. These figures highlight the significant advantages of using NIR-II emitting fluorophores for structural and functional imaging, especially in complex biological contexts. The extended imaging capabilities provided by NIR-II fluorophores offer exciting possibilities for studying a wide range of biological processes and disease conditions. Author permitted [409–411] by copyright 2020 American Chemical Society and Reprinted (adapted) with permission Copyright American Chemical Society.

A schematic depiction is presented, illustrating the comprehensive mechanism of incorporating an anticancer drug (DOX) into polymer-encapsulated superparamagnetic nanoparticles (SPMNPs). This diagram also outlines the concept of cancer cell eradication through a synergistic therapy approach involving the application of an alternating magnetic field (AMF). Notably, zeta potential measurements are employed to assess alterations in the surface charge of these nanoparticles resulting from the application of functional coatings. Reprinted adapted with permission from [407] Copyright © American Chemical Society.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Now, metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) are considered to have important scientific applications. Considering the toxicity of these nanomaterials is an important research issue for scientists. In this sense, an important solution to provide lower levels of toxicity and therefore to facilitate unimpeded use in products for human consumption is the green and eco synthesis of nanoparticles. Despite this naive approach, the biological synthesis of metal oxide NPs using microorganisms and plant extracts holds great promise for the fabrication of biocompatible and cost-effective particles with potential applications in healthcare and drug delivery. An important time of ​​attention is deadly cancer and therapies to control it, the introduction of nanotechnology is to improve current therapeutic practices. The oxides and NPs have been perceived as remedial factors with long obsolescence and therapeutic markers, and few immunogenic properties have been punched. Currently, biosynthesized metal oxide nanoparticles have been extensively studied and analyzed for early diagnosis and treatment of cancer, but their efficacy in clinical trials is unknown. It provides a comprehensive overview of recent research on the green synthesis of metal oxide NPs, characterization methods, biomedical and scientific applications globally reported, and indications for further improvements. Moreover, the scientific applications of nanoparticles, including silver (Ag), titanium (Ti), cerium (Ce), selenium (Se), zinc (Zn), iron (Fe), and copper oxide nanomaterials, were highlighted. The review ventilates the importance of environmentally and eco-friendly synthesis of metal oxides in terms of therapeutic, energetic, and environmental pharmaceutical applications using several novel approaches. This study will provide new knowledge on new cost-effective and nanomedicine drugs for future directions.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles, which have been used for a long time in history, have chosen characteristic images and inspired scientists to understand the structures and potential uses better. Nanoparticle-based science and technology are considered the most successful modern sciences, but they have been used since ancient times [1–3]. In the 9th century, the Mesopotamian artisans used to using nanoparticles (Au, Ag, and Cu) to produce foam on vessels. Nanoparticle research is interdisciplinary because it involves many different synthetic methodologies and characterization methods. Nanoparticle synthesis plays a crucial role in pioneering applications and relates to the problem of combining atoms or molecules into nanostructures of two desirable coordinate media, size, and shape. Many researchers are moving towards this molecular synthesis and using nanoparticles developed for new purposes [4–7]. Nanoparticles are defined as materials containing one or more sizes in the nanometer range. Some nanoparticles are rated below 1000 nm, but nanoparticles are generally placed between 1 and 100 nm. The (IUPAC) International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry explicates nanomaterials and nanoparticles as “particles of any shape with dimensions in the range of 1x10-9 and 1x10-7 m” [8–10]. The term “nanoparticles” can refer to materials that have properties different from those of bulk materials, and, for most fabrics, this phenomenon occurs in larger sizes less than 100 nm. “Nano” is sometimes used as a precursor for structures below 500 nm. This last definition refers to these terms, over the past decade; nano-biotechnology has undergone significant changes in many fields of study, such as engineering, electronics, biology, and many more [11–13]. They can provide you with various opportunities to develop tools, build new solutions, or customize results to get new nanotechnology programs and research just getting [14–16]. One of the most exciting things about this topic is the large number of nano-trees. These chemicals are thousands of times more massive than cells and are compatible with many proteins, enzymes, and molecular life [17]. Interest in metal oxide nanoparticles is growing, primarily as these nanoparticles are significantly used in manufacturing factory chemicals, chemical detectors, medical implements, metered-dose antifungal agents, disinfectants, fillers, pacifiers, catalysts, and semiconductors. Metal oxide nanoparticles attract attention mainly for bacterial, antifungal, and biochemical properties, which can be used for many biomedical purposes [18–21]. Nanomaterials are semiconductor metals accompanied by an individual optical, electrical, and magnetic effect that has been precluded in many implementations such as expanded build-out supercapacitors, near-infrared filters, magnetic storage media, biocatalysts, biosensors, bioenergy storage, etc. [22–26]. The prime limitations of the composition of nanoparticles are the control of the shape, size, and stems of the particles and the combination of different mechanisms developed to achieve this objective; the most widely studied methods include sonochemical, sol–gel, and laser cutting, electronic, chemical and synthetic processes [27–29]. Although nanoparticles are used in biomedicine applications, the main drawback in medicine is related to the potential toxic effects. NPS can also be harmful to mammalian cells, such as the biotic vertebrae. The primary operation of toxicity depends on the increase in ROS reactive oxygen species [33,34]. Therefore, these nanomaterials cause oxidative stress in the human lung's epithelial cells, contributing to the toxicity and the injury of DNA and wounds of vital organs.

Many of the dormant applications of the research are to nanomaterials covered in the research-based and prominent calendar, and there is no scarcity of assurance regarding the potential of nanometer-scale applied science to restructure life [35]. The first application is nano modular scale materials in systems where nano modular scale powders can be used freely without condensation or mixing [26–39]. For example, manufacturers and manufacturers of solar panels are now widely using zinc-titanium dioxide powder. Reflective paint was made using a nanometer measuring black titanium components. Wear durable nanostructured cutting tools; wear protective equipment used for several years. Some ships use bi-carbide coatings to increase capacity. Recently, nanoscale devices have been discovered [40–44]. Nano formed devices are widely used in information technology in combination with complex materials such as hard drives. Incorporating nanostructured materials and components at the nanoscale into complex systems, both magnetic data storage and silicon microelectronics create a vision for the future of science and technology at the nanoscale [45–47]. Structures called liposomes have been synthesized for better distribution of therapeutics in the biomedical field. Liposomes are lipid spheres approximately 100 nm in diameter [48–51]. They have been used in conjunction with cancer drugs to treat AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma [52–55]. To speed up separation and improve selectivity, several companies use magnetic nanoparticles to analyze blood, urine, and other body fluids. Other companies have created fluorescent nanospores and nanoparticle spots at the heart of new detection technologies [56–58]. These reagents are nano components used in new devices and systems to analyze infections and genetic diseases and detect drugs [59–61]. Numerous nanoparticle applications have appeared in the specialized market defense applications and the scientific and technological equipment market. Manufacturers of optical materials and electronic substrates, such as silicon and gallium arsenide, have begun using nanoparticles for chemical mechanical polishing [62–65]. Some nanoscale technologies seem to have been developing appropriate products for 3 to 5 years. For example, semiconductor crystals (point sums) of a nano chemical scale are currently being tested as a tool to study biological systems [66–69]. These spots become fluorescent after irradiation in specific light colors, depending on the nanosize and dimensions. In a natural reaction, quantum dots of various sizes may be applied to separate molecules, enabling researchers to monitor all molecules simultaneously during biological processes with a single scanning instrument [70,71]. The small-sized quantum dots are used as a safeguard aid for the faster and less time-consuming screening of DNA and antibodies than is possible with more traditional methods [72]. The applications of nanoparticles or nanomaterials are shown in Fig. 1 and were designed using basic version of BioRender.com and other necessary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The general approach for nanoparticle synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: All the nanoparticle sequencing cab is processed and classified into two techniques: the top-down style and the substratum −up procedure, the top-down design based on the miniaturization of the structure in the size of the couple. The top-down approach usually reaches the lithography or inkjet printing methods down to limits of about 50 nm. There are several other top-down methods, such as ion spray, laser ablation, pyrolysis, etc., which are known for minimal use. To further reduce the size limit, the general approach was assessed and well-established. After Feinman's discovery, scientists used atoms and molecules as building blocks to assemble and develop bottom-up large structures. In current years, remarkably progression has been made with the regulated production of size, shape, and composition of nanocrystals, enabling the adjustment of their properties [72]. The most widely used synthetic technique for nanoparticles is the colloidal technique, which gives uniform, monodisperse nanoparticles with immense dominance over the size, structure, shape, and composition of the nanoparticles [73]. In other methods, sol–gel and precipitation-based production methods are more concentrated due to their simplicity and regulate the water reaction, opening up possibilities for biomedical and biological applications. The plans include hydrothermal, micro emuNano-formedchemistry, and sample-based synthesis [74]. Metals oxides and their derivate have reported the use of antimicrobial coatings for years. The evidence for the benefit of aluminum, silver, copper, white metal, and mercury salts occasion back to primitive times, from the time of the shepherds, the great Roman people, and the time of the ancient Egyptians [75]. For centuries, doctors have used metal and salts to cleanse wounds and silver thread for the stitches of injured organs or body parts. Silver and silver salt are the most effective treatments for injuries and their use in the middle of the last century [76]. As a result, people have discovered antibiotics that differ in their chemical reactions and which are still pertinent in today's world. Since the 1940s, peculiarly through World War II, antibiotics have been an integral character of the treatment operation [77]. Nevertheless, bacterial infections have embellished a new threat, predominantly due to threadbare. Antibiotic resistance is widespread worldwide, and many superbugs pose a danger to inpatients and outpatients [78]. It gradually entering an era reminiscent of the pre-antibiotic age, a time when controlling broad-spectrum antibiotic-resistant pathogens will become increasingly challenging [79,80]. The systematic mechanism of Nanoparticles from bulk to powder is shown in Fig. 2 created with basic version of Biorendor tool.Fig. 3.
Three methods to synthesize nanoparticles: physical, chemical, and biological method [81].

Molecules that are organic and inorganic as reducing agents can be used to synthesize nano metals for chemical methods. Chemical removal of nanoparticles is done in two ways: the alcohol reduction method [82] and the polyol method [83]. Reduce the copper chloride to a size of 10 nm; 1-heptanol is used and significantly improved with oleylamine [84]. Silver nanoparticles are also synthesized using various organic media, such as n-butyl alcohol and ethylene glycol [85]. Polyvinyl pyrrolidone was used in the nitrogen atmosphere to synthesize copper nanoparticles [86]. Ethanol and water mixtures have been used to synthesize high-speed gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) using a plasma dissolution process [87]. In a one-step process, circular gold nanoparticles with a size measured from 23-79 nm and were synthesized in the appearance of a polymer matrix grafted with PEG-g-PVA [88]. The reduction of the silver nitrate leads to nano synthesis using ethylene glycol in PVP.

The oil in water or water in oil micro emulsion method has proven effective in the preparation of different types of materials [89]. A ZnO nanoparticle was synthesized employ the micro emulsion; subsequently, the important micro emulsion method was adapted to synthesize monodispersed zinc oxides [90]. Later, Nano particulate zinc oxides, which would then be used for thermoelectric applications, are synthesized continuously by the microfluidic system [91]. Using the polyoxyethylene ether, alcohol, polyoxyethylene ether, nonylphenol, and hexadecyl dimethyl ammonium bromide as a surfactant, BaTiO3 nanocrystals also used Nano emulsions process with the addition of a polymer matrix [92]. A highly efficient sodium heptane / AOT / (bi) continuous (BME) nanoemulsions can synthesize small mono-disperse platinum nanomaterials [93]. The reversed nanoemulsions process prepared zinc carbonate hydroxide composites with the appropriate particle size and morphology [94]. The platinum-ruthenium bimetallic transition nanoparticles were chemically reduced using sodium borohydride/isooctane/propanol Igepal CA-630/2 [95]. In Fig. 5, the Schematic of the microfluidic reactor shown the processing of synthesis of nanoparticles.

It is a technique that involves a biochemical coupling reaction of a solvent between a substrate and a catalyst concentration above that solvent's boiling temperature. With this method, Fe nanoparticles with an average nanoparticle size ranging between 35 nm were synthesized [96]. The technique is similar to the synthesis by thermal degradation of powdered carbonyls of different metallic nanoparticles.

Polymericnanoparticles are synthesized with controlled characteristics from iron-containing inputs such as iron Penta carbonyl, iron oleate, and iron oxide [97]. The complete combustion of boron oxide, urea, and copper nitrate, as well as h-BNN, develops Cu nanomaterials [98]. Copper nanomaterials have been developed by heat dissociation in copper oleate compounds [99].

The electrochemical method used propyl ammonium bromide salt as a stabilizing agent to synthesize titanium dioxide nanoparticles [100]. Meso porous (Au) gold was synthesized by soft patterns applying small stable electrochemical deposition block copolymers [101]. An electrochemical approach was used to synthesize silver nano dendrite with an electrochemical reaction of 0.01 mM AgNO3 [102]. The electrochemical method was used to synthesize zinc oxide-Au nanoparticles and modify an epidermis carbon electrode [103]. An electrochemical process based on the potentiostat polymerization of pyrrole and subsequent galvanic deposition synthesized the silver nanostructures [104]. TiO2 nanoparticles have been synthesized by the chemical method using the potentiostat [105]. Co nanoparticles have been synthesized through the reduction of [COCl4]2- by anthracene mediated by potentiostatic electrolysis, leading to ion formation during the dissolution of the cobalt anode.

It is a process in which broken metallic cables are used to synthesize metallic nanoparticles [106]. Metallic nanoparticles from copper and copper alloys are obtained by firing metal wires in aqueous solutions to synthesize highly conductive dyes [107]. The aluminum wire exploded to form aluminum nano silicon, which then assembled microporous membranes [108]. Nanoparticles of silver and copper were synthesized when a high voltage current passed through them, causing the wire to evaporate, a method called explosive wire technology. By adjusting the voltage conditions, the diameter of the nanoparticles was controlled [109]. The blasting process was used to produce nano-aluminum utilizing an atmosphere of nitrogen, argon, and argon. A system of wire plates and batteries for 12 was used to synthesize nanoparticles of 12 Ag-Au alloys with a diameter of nm utilizing jet cleaning [110].

To synthesize unique and novel metallic nanoparticles from the thermal plasma discharged, phosphorescent discharges are used. Thermal induction plasma has also been used to synthesize lithium metal oxide. Thermal plasma reactors are used for the synthesis of SiO2 nanoparticles [111]. The plasma method for nano production has vital ozone applications, material improvement, and water purification [112]. The one-step plasma synthesis method was used to synthesize carbon-coated crystalline Si nanoparticles (C-Si NPS), which have different fields of application, such as LEDs, biological markers, and nanocrystalline inks, etc.

It is a chemical process that combines the best quality and good performance. It is also used for thin-film production in the semiconductor industry; a nickel-based chemical, thermal vapor deposition catalyst, to synthesize Y-bond carbon nano oils.

The ZrO2 nanoparticles have been synthesized and then characterized by the microwave method at 80 °C [113]. To synthesize PbMoO4 byproducts, a microwave-assisted method had been used, which enhanced the photocatalytic performance and the nanoparticles of graphene (NP) from ultrasonic waves used in the pictorial removal of methylene blue (MB) dye under visible radiation [114].

The mushroom category increases the bulk mass production of nanoparticles [115]. When grown with elemental sulfur as the energy source, it decreases ferric ions in thiobacillus species presence. Soil polluted with metals was used to isolate a strain of bacteria (CS 11) for the extracellular production of silver (AgNPs) nanoparticles at ambient temperature [116]. Silver and gold nanoparticles were made using the aqueous extract of algal Turbinaria conoides [117]. Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by using extracellular agents derived from inaccessible types of bacteria such as Bacillus pumilus, Bacillus licheniformis, and Bacillus persicus [118]. The silver nanoparticles were produced by a novel and very new method using the phenol-dissolving broth culture cream [119]. The nanoparticles of silver were exceptionally produced by cell filtration of Myxococcus virescens [120] and Aspergillus tubingensis TFR-5 submitted in PubMed NCBI (GenBank Accession Number NCBI JQ675306) and both reported for the production of phosphor nanoparticles using three calcium phosphates [121] Hypocrea lixii has been used to trap and synthesize the Nickel Oxide nanoparticles [122] Colletotrichum sp. ALF2-6 was also used to synthesize nanoparticles of silver [123]. For the biosynthesis of Ag nanoparticles, Proteus mirabilis was used, and it’s devastating precursors were reported [124]. Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by virtue of rudimentary extracellular keratinase extracted from Bacillus safensis LAU 13 (GenBank accession number KJ461434) [125]. Two separate microbial strains from fungi reported were Penicillium expansum HA2N and Aspergillus terreus HA1NN fungi and developed using silver nanoparticles [126].

Green Fusion discovers the use of plant miscellany to synthesize nanoparticles in a speedy, one-step process. Ecological ideologies have other benefits over traditional producing nanoparticles [127]. Green synthesis is straightforward, includes a vascular response, is merely scalable, less toxic, biocompatibility is better suited for in vivo applications, ensures the nanometals nanoparticles, and is inexpensive [128,129]. The recovery of metal ions is achieved using several biomolecules such as amino acids, proteins, enzymes, etc. They are said to be not only environmentally friendly but also of medical importance [130]. Silver nanoparticles were obtained from the extract of leaves and stems of Piper nigrum [131]. Gold nanoparticles were obtained from diverse plant extracts of Punica granatum, Salvia officinalis, and Lippia citriodora [132]. The silver nanoparticles were made with Chrysanthemum Indicum, a simple, economical, and ecological method [133]. The magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4-NP) were produced to employ the Kappaphycus alvarezii algae as the provenance of stabilizers and reducing surrogates [134]. The microwave technique has been used to produce nanoparticles of silver using an aqueous extract from eucalyptus globule leaves [135]. Nanoparticles of gold were made using an ethanol extract from Syzygium aromaticum and examined for endemic anticancer prospective in the SU-DHL-4 cell line [136]. To transform Acinetobacter sp was activated as sludge was used within various physiological constraints for intracellular synthesis of metal nanoparticles. Synthesized (Au-NPs) gold nanoparticles are widely used in cancer treatment, drug delivery, and nano biosensors [137]. Nanoparticles were synthesized from silver nitrate with Sinapis arvensis [138]. Nanoparticles of silver were synthesized extracellularly with the gram-positive Streptomyces isolated from different rice fields [139]. The silver nanoparticles were obtained using a two-component plant extract of Piper nigrum [140].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advances in biological procedures of metal nanoparticles production over physical and chemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: Metallic nanoparticle biosynthesis is an efficient way of studying biological objects as a cheap way for a nano synthesizer. These procedures are fast, environmentally approachable, one-step, and simply scalable. Besides, the produced nanoparticles are constant, and mono disperses. Biosynthetic nanoparticles were considered more pharmacologically dynamic (possibly due to the binding of different pharmacologically dynamic residues) than nanoparticles produced by traditional procedures [141]. Studies have revealed that plants can reduce metal ions. Plants capable of hyper-accumulating and reducing metal ions are used to extract precious metals from the soil, a process called phyto-extraction [142]. Some conventional methods such as chemistry and physics have synthetic properties of metal nanoparticles, while these processes are laborious, costly, and environmentally harmful [143]. Plant species and microorganisms can also synthesize metal nanoparticles using metabolites and proteins from inorganic metal ions. Plants have demonstrated effectiveness over all other biotic systems such as bacteria, phytoplankton, microbes, fungi, bacteria, and human tissue as the affordable prices, short time-consuming, healthy, and essentially level the use plants in nanoparticles production, identifying them as a desirable phase for the synthesis of nanoparticles [144]. Plants and microorganisms can produce nanoparticles of metals from inorganic metal ions using the metabolites and proteins that they contain. Plants have additional benefits over other biotic systems such as diatoms, algae, yeasts, bacteria, human cells, and fungi as using plants in nanoparticle production is cheaper, shorter, safer, and easier to climb—an attractive stage for the synthesis of nanoparticles [145].

The antimicrobial nanomaterials are right with application in various biologically specialized disciplines, including specific alternative energy sources and environmental cleanup, as well as in biological applications and medicine. In all of the areas mentioned earlier, many materials and metals inseminated with nanomaterials are now in the marketplace [146]. Green nanotechnology is preferred in these areas in which sustainable development and protection play an important role. The potential release into the ecosystem of hazardous reducing agents and stabilizers or their byproducts can counteract the advantages of the material itself, even though the nanoparticles are being used successfully in the areas mentioned earlier. In recent times, good research interest has arisen in the word green nanotechnology. In comparison, due to better interaction or better acceptance of microorganisms, smaller size is much more beneficial [154]. Methods of synthesizing green nanoparticles are preferred and should be replaced by commercially available nanoparticles obtained by conventional methods. The nano filler is indeed very powerful for fungicidal adhesives since it does not burn or disperse over time and is not damaged when products are treated at elevated temperatures [230]. There is a broad range of gold nanoparticle applications, covering pharmaceutical drugs, oil refining, water treatment, and biomedical applications [225]. The actual uses of gold nanoparticles are gene therapy, fluorescence imaging, scanning, analysis, spectroscopy, and even antibacterial drugs [226]. Gold Nps have inherent bacteriostatic qualities, and while their use as antimicrobial agents is not as essential as silver nanoparticles, some microbes still have substantial antimicrobial activity [228]. Besides, the antibacterial activities and antimicrobial properties of green synthesized gold nanoparticles are very high in use [229]. There are much more significant cell limits among fungi, such as eukaryotic cells, than for microbes. Hence in their numerous bactericidal acts, the potency of gold nanoparticles is confirmed. Chloroauric acid is typically synthesized with plant-based gold nanoparticles as a substrate, which is then applied to these crude extracts to minimize it to metallic gold. Greenly synthesized plant-based gold nanoparticles have very less cytotoxicity compared to gold synthesized nanoparticles by chemicals. Nanoparticles are being used in the coloring of Nguyen acrylic glass and glass panels due to their active protection against bacterial fungi [232] and their antioxidant activity [12]. Likewise, plant-origin gold nanoparticles were used to coat various to obtain antimicrobial properties, including cotton, cotton, and charcoal [54]. New research has also shown that the antimicrobial activity of gold nanoparticles synthesized in green by organic compounds from natural products has improved.

Copper is the most reactive and poisonous precious metal and is around six times ecomonic less cheaper than silver and 24 times cheaper than gold, while the bactericidal properties of copper nanoparticles are high [193]. Research has also shown that copper nanoparticles would have a more substantial antibacterial effect than silver nanoparticles [193]. These biotechnological properties and other possible applications such as catalysts, antidepressants, antimicrobials, and energy applications have encouraged scientists to explore ways to reduce copper nanoparticles' toxicity and improve their safety in these areas and in clothes, copper nanoparticles are often used [233]. Although they are considered to have critical antimicrobial properties in 2019 [193]. Translucent copper nanoparticles are quite oxygen-responsive and oxidize rapidly in the air, reducing their potency against pathogens [234]. Sensitivity of oxygen turns them into spores and is dangerous to handle in the air. The synthesis of plants from copper nanoparticles can protect against oxidation and toxicity in human cells, while biomolecules from plant extracts form a non-toxic layer [197]. For example, biosynthetic copper nanoparticles have great potential for sanitary and coating applications and can sometimes be more efficient than conventional copper [235]. Copper sulfates and copper nitrates are perhaps the most common substrate salts used to synthesize green copper nanoparticles. Cu produced nanoparticle nanoparticles with Kobus magnolia leaf extract, the particle size of which varied depending on the intensity of nanoparticles. Plant nanoparticles of copper are considered against antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, antiparasitic, antioxidant, and effective pesticides [236]. Plant-based copper nanoparticle coatings have many potential applications in goods and services that are in immediate interaction with humans, such as antimicrobial latex foam for mattresses, because of their lower cost and safer properties or medical exfoliates and masks [237]. The absorption spectra of the metal oxide of silver and gold obtained from a plant extract have been reported [238]. ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of metallic nanoparticles and its antimicrobial mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: This very same anti-microbial function of the metallic nanoparticles that was not yet been reported and they are likely to have different effects on organisms that occur in the same way [241]. Many metals react with moisture and are then partially absorbed by smoke [242]. Exposure to the metal ion has a definite effect on the antimicrobial agent. This could attach to protein molecules, cellular membranes of microbes, and cytoplasmic membranes, contributing to the alteration of cells [243]. When metal ions penetrate bacterial cells, they appear to link to and diminish DNA and RNA particles, which interrupt and split their development in exchange [244]. For example, penalties prevent the absorption of phosphate by modifying it by triggering the release of essential nutrients. Examples are creatine monohydrate, xylitol, cysteine, and citric acid [245]. The metal nanoparticles themselves, in comparison to ions, present a risk to microbes. The high ratio of substrate to surface and volume defects could make metal oxides more responsive and more active in communicating with microbial species [246]. Size below 30 nm has been reported to be more appropriate for anchoring microorganisms and therefore invading them [15–17]. As a consequence, the capacity and moisture absorption of bacterial membranes is impaired, resulting in microbial extinction.

Nanoparticles of their small size, another critical factor in the antimicrobial efficacy of metal nanoparticles is the zeta potential or the surface charge of nanoparticles [247]. The nanoparticles have positive surface charges are generally more effective in antimicrobial tests [248]. With several anomalies, gram-positive and gram-negative infections commonly have an overwhelmingly negative influence mostly on cell membranes owing to tartaric acids and lipopolysaccharides [249]. The electrostatic interaction between negatively charged bacterial surfaces attracts positive metal nanoparticles for the bacterial laminate, and different action mechanisms continue to alter microbial life [250]. Another critical factor in the toxicity of metallic nanoparticles apropos microorganisms is their caliber to produce ROS [251]. That was probably because numerous nanoparticles can absorb and release radiation-generating particles [252]. ROS can destroy proteins and lipids, particularly DNA, as radicals and induce apoptosis [253]. In addition, mitochondrial respiratory injuries can result after adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is disrupted by reactive oxygen species [254]. To have a significant antibacterial effect nanoparticles must first bind to bacterial cell walls. However, when their environments are undesirable or when the type of bacteria is under strain, genetic makeup, and biological boundaries, such as mitochondrial biogenesis. The bacterium is a highly hydrated surface of secretions that primarily contains polyphenols. Protecting bacterial colonies from external damage such as extreme temperatures or antibiotic air has made it increasingly difficult to destroy these dinosaurs, which are increasingly sensitive to external influences [255]. In vitro, tests are usually performed under conditions that favor colonization. This indicates that strain enzymes are not triggered under these conditions; antimicrobial agents such as metal nanoparticles are more successful.

It is effortless to stop bacteria from growing than to kill a resistant colony. Thus, positive zeta potential is a significant undesirable potential in antimicrobial tests for nanoparticle overlay; a negative surface charge, on the other hand, decreases the relationship between negatively charged microbes and coated objects and puts forward [256]. On the other hand, fungal colonies usually cannot form biogas around them. Therefore, the antifungal metal nanoparticles can easily adhere to your cells. However, saprophyte cells have a strong cell wall or corpuscle; overall, it is extra challenging to enter eukaryotic cells than prokaryotic cells, as in bacteria [257]. For example, if a metallic nanoparticle manages to reach the interior by endocytosis [257], it can directly affect mitochondria, just like bacterial cells [258]. Likewise, it possesses electron transfer, ATP synthesis, and proton strength, influenced by metal nanoparticles and their molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of applications using plant extract-mediated synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: In environmentally sustainable applications and skin contact zones, bacteriostatic nanoparticle adhesives obtained by simple green fabrication techniques have tremendous potential [259]. Protocols for the green synthesis of metallic nanoparticles ensure that the nanoparticle's surface does not contain toxic chemicals necessary for their application in medicine [260]. Although plant-based antimicrobial coatings are not yet so standard, many cases have been reported [261].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and drawbacks of plant extract-mediated synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Three components must be present for the production of nanomaterials: a substrate, a reducer such as hydrazine hydrate, and a sculpting compound or stabilizing agent along with polyethylene oxide [262]. Depending on the chemicals used, it can also have serious toxic effects on humans and the environment. Therefore, the development of sustainable adaptation protocols requires an entirely new and environmentally friendly approach. The main advantage of the herbal mixture is that it retains residual toxins in the ingredients. The is the initial situation where people eat it directly with the cream or clothing, even when traces of toxic waste interfere with its safe use [263]. Also, considering the immense value of equipment and solvents, the expense of physical and chemical fabrication techniques is generally much higher. The quantity of byproducts developed is also significant, as they must be dealt with effectively and appropriately [264]. Plant-based synthesis and its protocols do not require isolation, identification, growth enhancement, propagation, preservation of microbes, all complex and challenging procedures, compared to other possible techniques such as microorganism-mediated propagation [265]. Also, the synthesis mediated by green plants tends to be faster and with shorter production times, suggesting better size options as it is a more straight forward method for the synthesis of green metallic nanoparticles [12,17,19,45,78]. The compared to conventional nanoparticle synthesis technologies, there are still barriers to be conquered. The predominant constraint was controlling the size and shape of the green metallic nanoparticles. Various phytochemicals predetermine the shapes and sizes with predetermined molecular sizes that are commonly found in plants. The composition of indistinguishable plants that extend in contrasting topographical domains or glean at other times of the year may differ in the agile ingredients. This inturn, may affects size, shape of the deposited nanoparticles. While industrial nanoparticles are generally modified, this will increase their valuation. It is sometimes more challenging to find timely appeal and promotion for plant-based metallic nanoparticles [266]. Plant extracts represent a considerable number of current components compared to chemical methods. It can be a possible problematic challenge and disinfect green synthetic nanoparticles from plant material [267]. And the benefits outweigh the negatives, encouraging researchers to improve their intervention methods on synthetic plants.

As discussed, the nanoparticle formation mechanisms are mainly related to the biomolecules present in plants. In particular, there are mostly molecules that act as a preservative and can recover alloys. A wide range of bioactive compounds and organic compounds taking responsibility for maintenance and restriction are documented in different reports, example of NADH-dependent reductase, alkaloids, carotenoids, terpenoids, sugars, astringency, flavonoids, and enzymes [268]. Most of these methodologies pertain to hydroxide ions group (–OH) substances, which are considered to be among the primary operational groups useful in determining different data elements nanomaterials [269]. Further research and understanding are needed to monitor the intermittent green transition mechanisms during breastfeeding. At this time, in addition to the toxicity and the combined effects of the extracted green nanoparticles, the size and distribution pattern of this breast must be controlled. In this sense, the path to chemical synthesis has evolved over the years and do still the primary reason researchers prefer it [270]. The processes by which plant extracts are used as precursors to produce nanoparticles are energy-efficient, affordable, and easily ascendable. In the scientific literature, there are indeed several studies that demonstrate that synthesis based on plant extracts are faster than other synthesis and can produce metallic nanoparticles with superintending morphology, structure, and dimensions. While the majority of environmental synthesis reports mention the production of metal nanoparticles, more and more research is being conducted into using plant-assisted synthesis methods to synthesize metal oxide nanoparticles. Due to the great diversity of plants, many herbs are available as nano plants grown almost anywhere in the world. All prices include energy-efficient units and orders based on their performance [271]. Green and clean technologies are of great importance for science and industry as live in a time when the human environment needs to be better adapted, and no development can take place. Synthetic methods based on plant extracts follow green chemical processes, and accordingly, the perspective of plants for the synthesis of nanoparticles should arise explored and exploited. Although some other provocations must be conquered in advance traditional synthetic methods can almost be replaced. The problems with biomolecules are still associated with the reduction and leveling of processes and their interaction [272].

The size, shape, crystalline size, and structure of the synthesized green nanoparticles are a challenge to control. Biosynthetic mechanisms can also be very different depending on the plant system. Also, in unquestionable circumstances, the molecule used can distinguish, while the catalysts experience the same. An important factor to acknowledge is the biosynthetic stability of nanoparticles. Green mixed nanoparticles are often enabled by entrapping plant macromolecules, as mentioned above [273]. It is necessary to ensure that nanoparticles endure steadily during storage without changing their structure inside or on the surface before they are put into practice. It is also essential that green nanoparticles do not have intrinsic properties on the work surface. The broad range of various shapes and sizes of metal nanoparticles and metal oxide have demonstrated functional antimicrobial effects on different pathogenic bacteria [274]. Significant amounts were synthesized using ecological means, such as syntheses mediated by plant extracts. Depending on the type of plant involved, the synergistic effects of nanoparticles and plant compounds bound to their surfaces are just about secure for use in medicine and physical intimacy areas and increased antimicrobial effects [275]. Green synthesis of metal oxides and transition metals, along with their coatings, offers promising alternatives to traditional antibiotics, potentially replacing some traditional Chinese medicine approaches. Table 1 summarizes some important examples of nanoparticle biosynthesis using plants.

Mapping/elemental composition of various tools such as EDS/EDXD, XRF (X-ray fluorescence), TEM, SEM and XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) can determine elemental composition and surface morphology [320]. Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectroscopy (ICP-MS) and Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry (AAS) instruments are also used to determine the elemental composition of NPs [321]. However, AAS cannot determine elemental composition when NPs are in solid form and must be dissolved in acids or bases before use. XPS can provide information on the composition and chemical state of elements present in NPs. EDHD technique is useful for elemental analysis and chemical composition of prepared MNPs [322]. XRF can be used to determine information about the elemental composition of a sample. Sample preparation for XRF analysis is simple, fast, and safe compared to other methods. It can detect up to 100 ppb (parts per billion) elements. It is a non-destructive method for analyzing solid samples using X-rays [323].

In recent years, nanoparticles have emerged as promising agents for combating inflammation. NPs possess a substantial surface-area-to-volume ratio, making them effective at intercepting inflammatory mediators, including cytokines and enzymes that promote inflammation, as well as other complement factors. Several metal-based nanoparticles, such as silver, gold, copper, and iron oxide nanoparticles, have been extensively studied for their remarkable anti-inflammatory properties. These studies have shed light on the mechanisms underlying the anti-inflammatory characteristics of nanoparticles [324]. Fig. 11 illustrates the mechanism of nanoparticles in anti-inflammatory systems. Inflammation is the body's immediate response to internal damage, infections, hormonal imbalances, or external factors like pathogenic microorganisms or environmental irritants. These triggers can lead to conditions such as obesity, food allergies, or interactions with environmental pathogens. The immune system responds by deploying immune cells with antigen receptors that recognize biochemical signals. Inflammation occurs when there is a disruption in the balance of these signals, which regulate the inflammatory response. [324]. Cell and tissue damage resulting from infection or injury triggers inflammation. This response involves the activation of macrophages and killer cells. Macrophages, large mononuclear phagocytes originating from the bone marrow and circulating as monocytes in the bloodstream, play a crucial role in the dynamic process of inflammation. They are a type of mobile white blood cell (leukocyte) [325–328], as reported in Nature.

These monocytes then move to different locations in many tissues and form macrophages. There are two types of macrophages: pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages, the production of which promotes inflammation, and M2 macrophages, which are alternately activated as an anti-inflammatory response and stimulate the remodeling of enlarged tissues and organs. Macrophages can support the inflammatory process by mediating changes between the two phenotypes depending on the disease delay. Through swelling, macrophages overcome damage to cells and tissues through phagocytosis and lead to inflammation through macrophage stimulatory activation signals [329–331,325]. Subsequently, these monocytes migrate to various tissues, where they differentiate into macrophages. There are two primary types of macrophages: M1 macrophages, which promote inflammation when activated, and M2 macrophages, which serve as an anti-inflammatory response and facilitate tissue and organ remodeling. Macrophages can dynamically switch between these two phenotypes depending on the specific circumstances, thus modulating the inflammatory process. Through processes like phagocytosis, macrophages play a crucial role in mitigating cellular and tissue damage, ultimately contributing to the resolution of inflammation by transmitting signals that stimulate their activation from the mechanish reported by scientisrs from the Fig. 12 and same reported many other scientists Engineering: A Case Study for Knowledge [329–331,325].

Nanomedicine places significant emphasis on the utilization of nanomaterials as contrast agents for both anatomical and functional imaging. Leveraging nanomaterials as contrast media facilitates the visualization of structures within the human body, aiding healthcare professionals in distinguishing healthy tissue in patients and recommending appropriate treatment strategies. Nanoparticles can be engineered with diverse contrasting properties, offering versatility in their applications [331]. The most common imaging modalities incorporating these nanomaterial-based contrast agents include computed tomography (CT), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), radioactivity-based imaging methods such as positron emission tomography (PET) and single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT), as well as fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging. The development of suitable materials is crucial for all of these techniques, as nanoparticles serve as contrast agents that enhance the visualization of biological tissues. In this context, nanoparticles can be tailored to target specific tissues and deliver high-contrast imaging outcomes [332].

Medicine encompasses the scientific, technological, and practical aspects of diagnosing, treating, curing, monitoring, predicting, and preventing diseases. One prominent field within medicine is nanomedicine, often associated with pharmaceutical formulations that involve introducing nano-sized particles, either soft or hard, into the human body for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. However, the realm of nanomedicine encompasses a much broader spectrum of research and development. What sets nanomedicine apart from other branches of medicine is its focus on the development and application of nanoscale materials and technologies that can operate in various ways [350,351]. Nanoscale objects exhibit properties that bridge the gap between molecular and bulk materials, and these properties can be found in both natural and synthetic materials. Typically, the field of nanoscience and engineering focuses on synthetic materials, while the study of nanoscale biological structures is often treated as a matter of characterization rather than considering biological properties [333]. The transient nature of “nanoscale” materials makes it challenging to establish strict definitions and numerical limits (e.g., larger than atoms or small molecules but smaller than 100 nm). Most importantly, nanoscale particles can exhibit unique properties that can be harnessed to create novel diagnostic and therapeutic effects [334]. Given the expansive scope of nanomedicine, it is expected to eventually encompass all facets of medicine. Moreover, for nanomedicine to be integrated into clinical practice, it must meet translation requirements, including safety, regulatory compliance, and ethical considerations [335]. The aspiration is to develop improved devices, drugs, and other applications of nanomedicine for highly specific, efficient, and personalized diagnosis and treatment, ultimately enhancing the quality of life for patients [336]. In this Nano Focus, the aim is not to provide a rigid definition of nanomedicine but rather to offer an overview of recent advancements, materials, and technologies in the field. Nanoparticles (NPs) are integral components of nanomedicine, with a wide array of nanoparticle types currently available. However, a standardized nomenclature is lacking in the literature, resulting in various terms like developed nanomaterials, non-biological compound drugs (NBCD), nanomedicine/nanomedicine, and more. Many nanomaterials can effectively replicate certain functions of biological macromolecules. Examples include lipid micelles [337], various polymeric nanostructures [338], protein constructs [339], ribonucleic acid nanoparticles (RNPs) [340], carbon dots (C-dots) [341], nanodiamonds (NDs) [342], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [343], graphene [344], as well as inorganic materials like mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNPs), superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) [345], quantum dots (QDs) [346], plasmonic nanoparticles [347], gold nanoclusters (GNCs) [348], up-converting nanoparticles (UCNPs) [349], and more.

Many of these nanoscale materials exhibit unique optical, electronic, and magnetic properties that are intricately tied to their size and shape. These properties are highly contingent on the methods used for their synthesis, purification, and characterization. [356,357]. It is widely acknowledged that even slight variations in size and shape can have a significant impact on the properties of nanoparticles (NPs). Consequently, achieving precision in the synthesis process is essential to obtain samples with precisely targeted distributions, which in turn enables the attainment of desired functions. It's imperative to establish correlations between the observed functions and specific NP properties. One noteworthy aspect in the field of nanomedicine is the absence of standardized characterization requirements for NPs prior to their application in humans [350]. This presents a critical area of focus for nanomedicine applications. The primary rationale behind this need is that the biodistribution and interaction of NPs with proteins are profoundly influenced by their size and surface characteristics. In heterogeneous samples, various NPs may exhibit distinct distribution patterns and potentially unwanted effects, including toxicity. In addition to characterization, there is a growing demand for the development of new and improved techniques for NP separation and purification. This is crucial for producing optimal samples for applications in nanomedicine and for gaining insights into NP behavior within the human body, a critical factor in the design of effective NP formulations for medical purposes [351].

While there is a pressing need for standardizing characterization methods for nanoparticles (NPs), they hold great promise for enhancing disease detection and diagnosis. Firstly, the development of intelligent NPs allows for targeted delivery and the provision of critical information about the local environment once administered within the body. This information can be instrumental in creating detailed images of fine anatomical structures within organs, labeling specific tissues with markers, and enabling localized measurements of concentrations of molecules of interest. Such capabilities are invaluable in the direct analysis of diseases within the human body. Secondly, NPs play a pivotal role in advanced diagnostic technologies, enabling the rapid analysis of samples collected externally from the body, such as blood or tissue. This facilitates the swift detection of biomarkers and molecular changes. The capacity to simultaneously analyze multiple biomarkers significantly enhances diagnostic accuracy. Moreover, multifunctional or therapeutic NPs are being developed to not only diagnose but also treat and monitor therapeutic effectiveness [352]. NPs are increasingly finding applications in the treatment of diseases, serving as carriers for pharmaceutical drugs, bioactive materials, or integral components of therapeutic strategies. NP-based delivery systems possess a unique ability to traverse biological barriers. As a result, NPs can infiltrate tumors through their locally permeable vasculature and remain within the tumor microenvironment due to limited lymphatic drainage. This passive targeting phenomenon is known as the Enhanced Permeation and Retention (EPR) effect. The literature provides substantial evidence regarding the effectiveness of ligand/receptor-mediated active targeting in comparison to passive targeting. It is crucial to ensure that each vector reaches its intended site before binding to receptors on cell surfaces or being retained due to other effects [353].

And to utilidzing nanomedicine for disease diagnosis and treatment, it is essential to assess the effectiveness and safety of nanoparticles (NPs) within biological systems. A key consideration is what happens to carrier particles during drug delivery or tissue analysis once NPs are administered into the body. These particles can be cleared through renal or hepatobiliary mechanisms. If successful clearance does not occur, the long-term fate of NPs remains uncertain. They may undergo breakdown and excretion through the kidneys due to their small size, allowing them to pass through the kidney's filtering slits. Alternatively, NPs can accumulate in various organs and interact with non-target cells [354]. The in vivo destiny of NPs is a dynamic process, emphasizing the need to comprehend nano-biokinetics (nano-pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics), which may relate to the distinct and intriguing toxicological responses associated with NPs. It's essential to acknowledge that nanomedicine extends beyond colloidal materials and the technologies designed for in vivo applications. Nanomedicine encompasses diverse developments that go beyond the concept of a magic particle sphere. This includes the creation of novel scaffolds and surfaces for engineered sensors, implantable systems, and electronics to support tissue regeneration, such as regenerative medicine. While many of these concepts are still in early developmental stages, some have already found their way into clinical practice. The need for higher sensitivity in medical imaging has prompted nanomedical researchers to explore nanoparticles as contrast agents for X-ray imaging. For instance, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are often coated with specific molecules like folic acid to highlight distinctive tissue structures. Smaller structures known as gold nanoclusters (NCs) are also being developed as contrast agents for computed tomography (CT) imaging [355]. Researchers have demonstrated that these NCs offer excellent contrast for CT images. And can be used for molecular imaging, as they exhibit red fluorescence emission [356]. These NCs also have the advantage of penetrating and remaining in tumors while being small enough for renal clearance, making them promising candidates for future clinical use. NPs composed of high atomic number materials, including gold, are also well-suited for quantum dot (QD) applications. An example is NaGdF4-based up-converting NPs (UCNPs). These NPs not only provide CT contrast but can also be visualized optically [357]. While offering valuable contrast capabilities for CT, the potential toxicity of NPs plays a crucial role in clinical applications discussed in the below Fig. 13 .

Over the past 25 years, researchers have extensively explored various nanoparticles and complexes for their potential as MRI contrast agents, leading to the approval of several formulations for clinical use. These contrast agents typically comprise transition metals, lanthanides, iron oxide nanoparticles, and more recently, ferrite nanoparticles. Transition metals and lanthanides, with gadolinium ions (Gd3+) being a prominent representative, are commonly employed as T1 contrast agents due to their ability to extend longitudinal relaxation times, enhancing image contrast [358]. A new generation of T1 contrast agents involves Gd complexes that are immobilized within various nanostructured materials, including nanoporous silica, dendrimers, perfluorocarbon nanoparticles, and nanotubes. Iron oxide nanoparticles with a total diameter exceeding 50 nm are also harnessed as MRI contrast agents, as they effectively reduce T2* relaxation times in tissues like the liver, spleen, and bone marrow through selective uptake by mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS) cells. Iron oxide nanoparticles smaller than 50 nm can be utilized for imaging lymph nodes, and depending on the size of the iron core and its concentration, these smaller nanoparticles can extend T1 relaxation times, further enhancing their utility [359]. Iron oxide nanoparticles can be functionalized with bioactive materials, enabling targeted imaging through site-specific accumulation. Beyond their role as mere MRI contrast agents, smart nanoparticles are designed to respond to changes in the tumor microenvironment, facilitating both therapeutic and diagnostic functions [360]. Activation triggers for these nanoparticles commonly involve pH, temperature, redox reactions, metabolites, ions, proteases, ultrasound, and light. Utilizing iron-based magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) as contrast media in MRI offers several advantages, including the ability to control the concentration of the imaging agent during MNP synthesis [361]. The surface properties of MNPs can be tailored to modulate their circulation and retention time within the body. Iron-based MNPs exhibit unique magnetization properties that significantly impact the reduction of transverse relaxation time (T2), resulting in enhanced image contrast at very low concentrations during MRI. This property allows for the modification of the relaxation properties of protons (1H) due to the magnetic behavior of MNPs [362]. Iron-based MNPs, known for their low toxicity and high biocompatibility, are widely employed as specific biomarkers in the field of MRI oncology. MNPs serve as multifunctional agents, seamlessly integrating diagnostic and therapeutic properties. These versatile nanoparticles can be fine-tuned for specific biological applications, such as magnetically targeted hyperthermia, gene delivery, cell sorting, and drug delivery, demonstrating their potential in various medical contexts [363] and shown in the Fig. 14 .

The term “fluorescence” encompasses a range of spectroscopic methods used in acquiring images by measuring various parameters, including intensity, decay time (lifetime), polarization, and the study of effects related to resonant (dynamic) energy transfer, photo-induced extinction, or electron transfer. Optical imaging was historically limited to resolutions of a few hundred nanometers until around 1995 [364,365]. The substantial increase in resolution can be attributed to the emergence of nanoscale fluorescence imaging techniques like STED, PALM, or STORM, all of which rely on laser technology [366]. Turning the focus to bioimaging based on synthetic fluorescent probes and nanoparticles, three distinct techniques can be identified. In its simplest form, a potent fluorophore or fluorescent nanoparticles are introduced into cells, allowing for their visualization. These fluorophores and nanomaterials are designed solely to induce fluorescence in cells or tissues, without any particular affinity for specific sites or responsiveness to the presence of chemical species, such as ions or organic molecules. The second technique is termed “targeted bioimaging” [367], which enables the detection of specific domains or species, including immune labeling or fluorescent in situ hybridization. This is achieved through the use of properly functionalized fluorophores or nanoparticles, featuring receptors, ligands, antibodies, or oligomers, designed to recognize specific homologs. Examples include the targeting of tumor markers, genes, mitochondria, membranes, or amyloid plaques in tissues related to Alzheimer's disease [368]. Fluorescence does not pertain to a single spectroscopic method but encompasses a variety of techniques used for imaging based on measurements of intensity, lifetime, polarization, as well as the examination of phenomena related to energy transfer, quenching, or photo-induced electron transfer. Prior to 1995, optical imaging was constrained to resolutions of a few hundred nanometers, but the advent of nanoscale fluorescence imaging methods, such as STED, PALM, or STORM, has significantly improved resolution [369]. While both chemiluminescence and bioluminescence can produce images, chemiluminescence requires the addition of reagents and is typically unidirectional, only monitoring changes in analyte concentration. Additionally, both methods are subject to time-dependent conditions, as shown in Fig. 15
[370].

Nanoparticles find versatile applications in the detection of molecules, cells, and tissues outside the human body [371]. In this diagnostic role, nanoparticles serve to identify unique biological molecules present in biological fluids relevant to a patient's health [372]. Nanoparticles effectively act as transducers and are coated with ligands, making them valuable tools for biosensing single biological molecules from in vitro sensing applications in liquid environments [373–375]. As an illustration, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been engineered with linkers that selectively bind to complementary proteins. The presence of these proteins triggers the cross-linking of nanoparticles, resulting in agglutination. This controlled agglomeration can be observed colorimetrically through changes in the solution's color [376]. These concepts have evolved rapidly, leading to advancements in technologies like the colorimetric detection of DNA. This diagnostic approach based on AuNPs has progressed to the extent of testing patient samples and has now found application in clinical settings. The integration of nanotechnology into the diagnostic process has substantially enhanced its capabilities by lowering the detection limit, enabling the detection of a multitude of biological targets, and facilitating high-sensitivity multiplexing [377].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vivo Treatment based on the particle delivery
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have been specifically engineered for the treatment of various diseases, with cancer being one of the most prominent targets. Extensive literature contains substantial in vivo datasets obtained from animal studies. Some nanoparticle-based formulations for cancer treatment have received approval from regulatory authorities and have made their way into hospitals, demonstrating a significant reduction in side effects when compared to the parent drugs. However, it is noteworthy that while they often exhibit reduced side effects, their therapeutic efficacy has not shown substantial improvement or has only marginally improved. Consequently, there are ongoing efforts to develop systems that can deliver enhanced therapeutic efficacy. A key development in this endeavor involves refining the design of nano drug delivery systems by precise control of material functions and properties [378].

The process of introducing anti-tumor drugs into a solid tumor is characterized by five crucial stages, collectively known as the CAPIR cascade, which includes blood circulation, tumor accumulation, penetration into the tumor, cellular internalization, and intracellular drug release. Therefore, the overall therapeutic effectiveness of nanomedicine is contingent upon its performance at each of these steps [379]. A successful nanodrug formulation that effectively triggers the entire CAPIR cascade should exhibit a high therapeutic index [380]. Consequently, such nanomedicine must possess the characteristics represented by the acronym 2R2SP, which stands for delayed anti-delayed drug release (2R), surface stealth vs. adhesive (2S), and tumor penetration (P). The 2R component underscores the crucial attributes of nanomedicine with regard to the loaded drug. It necessitates that the drug should be delivered to the intracellular target for therapeutic action while securely holding onto the drug without premature release during its transport through the bloodstream and tumor tissues. Similarly, 2S encompasses the desirable surface properties of nanoparticles. These nanoparticles should remain stealthy when circulating in the bloodstream to facilitate passive deposition in tumors but become adhesive when they encounter tumor cells, promoting efficient cellular uptake. In addition to the properties of 2R and 2S, the nanomedicine must be capable of penetrating the tumor tissue to eliminate tumor cells located away from blood vessels. This can only be achieved if the nanomedicine possesses 2R2SP properties, enabling the timely and targeted delivery of active drugs to engage with the CAPIR cascade, ultimately leading to high overall therapeutic efficacy and favorable outcomes [381]. Consequently, the current emphasis on developing high therapeutic index nanodrugs centers on adjusting the fundamental physicochemical properties of nanoparticles, encompassing “size, surface characteristics, and stability” (the nano 3S property) to attain 2R2SP properties, thus effectively executing the CAPIR cascade and reported by scientists from the Fig. 16 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Delivery of Macromolecular biopharmaceuticals across biological barriers
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomedicines are currently under development for the diagnosis and treatment of a wide range of conditions, including infectious, neurodegenerative, and cardiovascular diseases [382,383]. While traditional cancer drugs are typically small organic molecules, there is a growing emphasis on macromolecular biopharmaceuticals, encompassing polypeptides and polynucleotides. However, the substantial size and complexity of these biopharmaceuticals present significant challenges when it comes to their safe and effective utilization. Issues such as rapid clearance and limited metabolic stability need to be addressed. Moreover, these compounds often face difficulties in permeating biological barriers, such as cell membranes or epithelial tissues [384]. Nonetheless, the use of macromolecular biopharmaceuticals holds promise not only in alleviating symptoms but also in potentially curing or preventing diseases. This can be achieved by directly addressing hormonal imbalances (e.g., insulin in diabetes) or by correcting, preventing, or genetically reprogramming malfunctioning cells. One important future application of nanomedicine lies in the delivery of these macromolecular biopharmaceuticals, ideally via non-invasive methods that eliminate the need for needles. This approach leverages the ability of nanoparticles to stabilize and protect therapeutic payloads as they traverse epithelial tissue barriers, such as those found in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, skin, or lungs.

Nanotechnology yields positive outcomes in enhancing sensorimotor and cognitive functions across a spectrum of neurological conditions, including stroke, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, Alzheimer's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and spinal muscular atrophy [385]. In combination with stem cell therapy, nanoparticles significantly enhance the efficacy of cell-based treatments, capitalizing on the unique properties of nanomaterials [386]. Nanoparticles have the capacity to engage with neurogenic stem cell niche factors, thereby facilitating both endogenous and exogenous stem cell self-renewal, proliferation, and differentiation. The design of nanoparticles has a crucial impact on their compatibility with subsequent applications. The shape of these nanoparticles plays a pivotal role in biological functions such as drug delivery, half-life, endothelial distribution, and targeting ability. Smaller nanoparticles enable faster drug release owing to their elevated surface area-to-volume ratio, whereas larger nanoparticles facilitate slower drug release through quicker polymer degradation [387]. The surface charge and hydrophobicity of nanoparticles influence their biodistribution, circulation time, and potential toxicity. Positively charged nanoparticles offer benefits in imaging, gene transfection, and drug delivery, but they may also exhibit increased cytotoxicity. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) stand out as ideal candidates for delivering substantial quantities of antibiotics due to their biocompatibility and customizable size. The shape, size, and surface properties of AuNPs modulate their interactions with cells. During the self-assembly of AuNPs, functional groups become exposed on the surface, significantly enhancing the activity of these functional groups. In particular, surface-bound oxygen catalyzes the oxidation reaction of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, a pivotal energy cofactor in enzymatic processes [388]. This oxidation of NADH drives cellular respiration and metabolic activities that play a role in myelination. AuNPs hold the potential to support remyelination therapy, aiding the restoration of compromised functions in the presence of neurodegenerative diseases and improving patients' quality of life, possibly even reversing disease progression [404–410]. AuNPs also have the capacity to enhance drug delivery for promoting neuronal cell potentiation. For instance, the combination of AuNPs with stem cell therapy can heighten specific cell targeting and further stimulate the self-renewal, proliferation, and differentiation of endogenous and exogenous stem cells [389–395].

Nanomedicine continues to witness the emergence of novel nanoparticles (NPs) for various applications, notably in the realms of drug delivery, combating antibiotic resistance, advanced imaging, diagnostics, and therapies for cancer. Recent research has highlighted the efficacy of utilizing multiple nanomaterials, such as nanodiamonds (NDs) and proteins, or a single nano platform engineered to carry multiple therapeutic agents for enhanced tumor imaging and treatment. NP-based drug delivery strategies entail the deliberate, biomimetic, and methodical design of optimal combinations of therapeutics. Nanomedicine has ushered in substantial advancements, spanning fundamental and translational research, in cellular studies, preclinical investigations, and clinical trials. Nevertheless, much of the emphasis has been placed on cancer diagnosis and treatment, creating an imbalance that is expected to be addressed in the near future. Researchers are increasingly directing their efforts toward addressing other critical medical challenges, such as antibiotic resistance and the development of artificial organs. The adaptability and versatility in the design of nanomaterial platforms render nanomedicine valuable in these domains, enabling multimodal therapeutic approaches and highly specialized, versatile designs tailored for specific biological applications. While nanomedicine presents promising solutions for a wide array of medical issues, it has also brought forth scientific challenges, particularly stemming from a limited understanding of nanomaterial behavior within living organisms. Ongoing foundational research dedicated to addressing these questions is bringing us closer to overcoming these challenges and achieving tangible medical solutions in the field of nanomedicine.
